votes.reason: The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by checking if all elements in the list are strictly less than the threshold.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by checking if all elements in the list are strictly less than the threshold.
The Python code correctly implements the Coq specification by checking if 'res' equals the boolean result of whether all elements in 'l' are strictly less than 't'.

The Coq spec uses forallb with Nat.ltb to check if all elements are below threshold t, returning a boolean. The Python code uses all() with x < t, which produces the same boolean result for all concrete list inputs and threshold values.

The Coq spec uses forallb with Nat.ltb to check if all elements are strictly less than t, returning a boolean. The Python code uses all() with x < t, which also checks if all elements are strictly less than t. Both return true/false based on the same condition, so they produce the same concrete results for all inputs.

The Coq spec uses forallb with Nat.ltb (strict less-than), and the Python code uses all() with x < t (also strict less-than). Both return a boolean indicating whether all elements in the list are strictly less than the threshold t. The concrete behavior matches for all inputs.
It checks that res equals whether all elements of l are less than t, matching Coq’s forallb with Nat.ltb, including empty list behavior.

Both define res as the result of checking that every x in l is less than t (forallb with Nat.ltb vs Python all with <), including empty lists; types align on intended domain of natural numbers and booleans.
It checks res equals the forallb of Nat.ltb x t over the list (all elements x < t); behavior (including empty list) matches for natural-number inputs.
